speaker: interviewee
text: Right at me. He said, I'm a [INAUDIBLE] member. He said I can work at home ever what I want to do. And that picture was taken about in '52, '53 I think. I can remember quite well. It was taken in the garden.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my marriage certificate what has been made. Excuse me. Thank you. [DRINKS] Thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date is on the marriage certificate?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1941, December on the 7th. And we are here. It's Polgar. But before that is how you can see it was Pulitzer. Why we did that to Polgar, because that time already it was a Jewish name. That why became Polgar.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there are two names on that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, on there. Pulitzer-- Miksa Pulitzer and Miksa Polgar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Edith, who is this a photo of?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are my parents. My father is Hugo Spielmann. My mother is Julia Spielmann. And that picture has been taken in [INAUDIBLE] in Baden in his workshop in 1938.

speaker: interviewee
text: That I couldn't tell you. Somebody. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Edith, what is this a photo of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wedding in 1941, December on the 7th. That's outside the synagogue. And the ceremony is already finished. On the right hand side is my sister, Greta.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's a photo of you and your husband?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the synagogue?

speaker: interviewer
text: Edith, who is this a photo of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my husband, my daughter Judy, and myself in 1946.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the street at [INAUDIBLE], in Gyor.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, Greta. This her late husband. His name is [INAUDIBLE], and that photo was taken in 1960.

speaker: interviewee
text: In my place. At 3 Ocean Street [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo is my brother-in-law. His name is Loewy [INAUDIBLE]. And my sister Herta. Was taken in 1960 in my place, 3 Ocean Street, Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this photo are my daughter and my nephews. In the middle, that is Judy Polgar. On the left side, the little one is Tommy Loewy. On the left side next him is Peter Loewy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the right-hand side is [INAUDIBLE] Weisz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that picture have been taken Sydney on the airport in 1967 when my daughter flew away for her 21st birthday to Europe.

speaker: interviewee
text: In that photo has been taken on a wedding. That's my husband, myself, and my daughter who is around 18 years old in that picture. Was taken in Sydney in Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son-in-law, my only son-in-law, whose picture was taken in America in Baltimore in 1983. He was studying for his [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is-- what's his name? George Szwarc. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Edith, who-- who do we have here?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my small family. To the right of me in the green one is my daughter Judy. Next to it is Gregory Szwarc. That's my grandson. On my left is my only one granddaughter Karen Bauer who is already married. The maiden name Karen Szwarc.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Edith, if I can just ask you now that you have your family with you, you lived through so much during the Holocaust. How do you think it affected your life afterwards and the way you brought up your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it affected me very much so. But when you have a new life to start and you're coming to a country who is full with roses and everything is full of roses, then you try to be optimistically-- to bring your child up optimistically in a new country is a good life and a good education.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what are-- what are the things that you think you wanted to give Judy that you didn't have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything love and everything what I didn't have in my childhood later on to give her. Everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever tell her about you experiences.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. My daughter, my grandchildren. Everybody knows what I went through and what we did and what I have done after, before, and now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Judy, if I can ask you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Growing up with parents who lived through the Holocaust-- what are your memories of-- of your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: My childhood? My childhood was always very happy. I grew up in my early stage of my life in Hungary, which at that stage was communist. So in that respect, it was rather gray. But there was always a lot of love, and I always remember being loved and nurtured and being very special. And I suppose being an only child as well, that had a lot to do with it. As far as the Holocaust is concerned, when I was very young of course, my parents didn't talk about it. But as I got older, my mother never hid the fact that she was at Auschwitz and my father was also in the working concentration camp and also, plus the fact that I had a younger-- an older sister rather, who died in a concentration camp. And I was very much aware of what my parents have gone through. And in lots of ways, that has influenced my life as well. My Judaism-- as far as bringing up my children to be Jews and to know their Judaism and to be proud of the fact that they are Jews and make sure-- we're trying to make sure-- instill in them the pride and make sure that nothing what has happened to my mother and my father that they would have to go through.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, same with mom. Like, they try to-- I guess mom tried to pass on what nana started off with. And when the Holocaust-- like, as nana said, we were told everything. Plus, it was reinforced in schoolwork. We went-- I went to Masada where it was all reinforced and we learnt about it. And I could understand a bit better what nana went through, but not everything because you have to be there. And also the fact that going to a Jewish day school, starting off, and also being proud to be Jewish, I think it's very important as well. So I think-- I think that's the most important thing at the moment, to be proud to be Jewish, not to hide the fact.

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess it is very important-- and that's something that's been instilled in all of us-- to remember to be Jewish and to be proud of the fact, And I think one of most important things is that all of us-- like my generation-- can learn and remember so that when we have children and our children have children that everyone knows and that no one ever forgets because I think that was part of the reason of the Holocaust, that the Jews became so complacent with where they were in the world that they thought no one would never harm them. But obviously, that's not the case. I think that's a very important thing to pass on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Edith, if there's anything that you could pass on to this generation--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I only can pass on that in words what I feel [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] that I'm very lucky to have a family like that who is so close to me. We love each other. We protect each other. And perhaps for in the future, it will be never happen again that my family goes through what I went through. That why I brought Judy to Australia, and Judy is in Australia with her children and the whole family, that we having a future and will be never happen again what happened to us.
